nativeRange.habitat: Hybrid/cultivar of horticultural origin; precise wild parentage is not formally documented but hobby literature and the primary product source (Buce Plant) commonly attribute the lineage to the Cryptocoryne wendtii complex. C. wendtii itself is endemic to Sri Lanka (Wikipedia), while Buce Plant attributes the Pink Flamingo cultivar specifically to Borneo. The two origin claims are not reconciled in available sources; the broader Cryptocoryne genus is naturally distributed across tropical India, Southeast Asia, and New Guinea in slow-flowing streams, rivers, and seasonally inundated forest pools.

decorPreferences: iron-rich root tabs near the root zone
high light to bring out intense pink/coral coloration
stable, soft, slightly acidic water to minimize crypt melt

avoidWith: Silver Dollars and other plant-eating fish

summary: A rare specialty Cryptocoryne cultivar prized for its ruffled leaves that emerge blood-red and mature to vibrant pink and coral tones. Demanding light, iron, and stable soft water for full color expression.
careNotes: Cryptocoryne 'Pink Flamingo' is a premium specialty cultivar in the planted-tank hobby, distinguished by ruffled leaves that emerge blood-red and gradually lighten to vivid pink and coral tones — a coloration unusual in the otherwise green/brown/bronze Cryptocoryne genus. Its precise taxonomic origin is not formally documented; it is most commonly described as a hybrid or designer cultivar associated with the Cryptocoryne wendtii lineage. Buce Plant attributes the cultivar to Borneo, while Wikipedia documents C. wendtii (the presumed parent species) as endemic to Sri Lanka — this discrepancy is noted but unresolved, as Pink Flamingo's actual hybrid parentage is not formally published. Mature plants form compact rosettes on the smaller end of the Cryptocoryne range — typically 5-15 cm tall — making this a midground (sometimes foreground) specimen rather than a large background rosette. To bring out and hold the signature pink coloration, the prevailing hobby advice is HIGH lighting (Buce Plant: low-to-medium light is tolerated but the deepness of pink color scales with light intensity and CO2 availability), supplemental CO2 (Buce Plant: 'not necessary but recommended' — strongly advised for intense color and faster growth), iron-rich fertilization (iron-dosed root tabs and water-column micronutrients are widely cited as critical for pink pigment expression), and soft, slightly acidic water (Buce Plant: pH 6-7). Like other Cryptocorynes, this is a HEAVY ROOT FEEDER — nutrient uptake happens primarily through the root zone, so a nutrient-rich substrate (aqua soil) and regular root tabs are strongly recommended. Plant by burying roots in substrate up to the crown (where leaves emerge from the rhizome); do NOT bury the crown itself. CRYPT MELT WARNING: Pink Flamingo is reported to be more sensitive to crypt melt than common Crypts like C. wendtii — Buce Plant explicitly warns 'unstable parameters will result in melt and rotting.' Newly planted specimens or those exposed to a sudden parameter shift frequently shed most or all of their leaves and may take 4-8 weeks to recover. Stable parameters and avoiding relocation once planted are essential — Buce Plant emphasizes 'Cryptocoryne prefer not to be moved once planted.' Cross-source confirmation: Buce Plant (user-verified primary) confirmed pH 6-7, low-to-medium light tolerated (pink color requires more light + CO2), CO2 'not necessary but recommended', moderate growth rate, easy difficulty once established, rhizome-cut propagation, Araceae family, Borneo origin attribution, and melt sensitivity. Wikipedia (Cryptocoryne wendtii, parent species) corroborated Araceae/Alismatales taxonomy, classification as an easy and robust Cryptocoryne, propagation by runners, variable appearance under different light conditions, and native range (Sri Lanka — conflicting with Buce Plant's Borneo claim). Aquarium Co-Op was attempted as a second product source but returned a redirect loop (HTTP) and was abandoned per fail-fast protocol. Lower-stakes fields (temperature 22-28 °C, gH 2-15, kH 1-10, tank size, decor preferences, compatibility lists) are biologically reasonable for a wendtii-lineage cultivar but rely on hobby-standard ranges rather than per-cultivar product confirmation.
breedingNotes: Propagated primarily by rhizome cutting/division — divide a healthy rhizome into sections, each retaining leaves and roots, and replant. Buce Plant explicitly notes 'cut by rhizome' as the propagation method. Runner (stolon) production typical of C. wendtii (the presumed parent species, propagated by runners per Wikipedia) is reportedly slower for this cultivar than for the vigorous wild species. Seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium and would require emersed flowering.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: User-verified working URL. Primary product page. Confirmed Araceae family, pH 6-7, low-to-medium light tolerated (pink color intensity scales with light and CO2), CO2 'not necessary but recommended', moderate growth rate, midground placement, rhizome-cut propagation, easy-once-established difficulty, and Borneo origin attribution. Explicit melt warning: 'unstable parameters will result in melt and rotting.' Page is thin on temperature, gH/kH, adult size, and lifespan.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Cryptocoryne wendtii — presumed parent species)
additional.notes: Parent-species cross-check (lineage commonly attributed in hobby literature). Confirmed Araceae family, Alismatales order, classification as 'an easy plant to grow and one of the most robust Cryptocoryne species,' propagation by runners, variable appearance under different light conditions, and native range Sri Lanka (conflicting with Buce Plant's Borneo attribution for the Pink Flamingo cultivar specifically). Wikipedia does not document the Pink Flamingo cultivar by name.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op (attempted)
additional.notes: Second product source attempted for cultivar-specific cross-verification. WebFetch returned a redirect loop (too many redirects exceeded). Abandoned per fail-fast protocol. No data extracted.
